ui_page.title: Wedding Budget Calculator | Plan Your Perfect Day

expected_source.title: Wedding Budget Calculator | Plan Your Perfect Day

comparisons.expected_content: Wedding Budget Calculator Tips Blog About Privacy Terms Contact Smart Wedding Planning Plan your dream wedding without breaking the budget.
Enter your total wedding budget and get an instant breakdown for venue, catering, photography, dress, rings, and more.
Adjust, customize, and print your plan in minutes.
Start the calculator Read money-saving tips ✔ Live category breakdown ✔ Tracks over / under spend ✔ Printable budget summary Wedding budget snapshot Your day, your numbers.
See a clear breakdown before you sign any contracts.
Planned budget $25,000 Saved by smart planning 10–20% Wedding Budget Calculator Start with your total budget, then fine-tune each category to match your priorities.
We’ll keep track of your planned spend, recommended amounts, and how much you have left.
Set your total budget Total wedding budget $ Tip: You can adjust category percentages below to match your vision (for example, a smaller guest list but higher photography budget).
Customize category percentages These percentages are a common starting point.
Feel free to tweak them.
Venue % Catering % Photography % Dress % Rings % Misc % Total allocation: 100 % Reset to recommended percentages 3.
See your smart breakdown Enter your planned spend for each category to see if you’re over or under budget.

Venue Ceremony & reception space, décor, rentals Recommended: $0 Your plan $ Over / Under: $0 Catering Food, cake, bar service, tasting Recommended: $0 Your plan $ Over / Under: $0 Photography Photo, video, second shooter, album Recommended: $0 Your plan $ Over / Under: $0 Dress & Attire Dress, suit, tailoring, accessories Recommended: $0 Your plan $ Over / Under: $0 Rings Engagement ring, wedding bands, resizing Recommended: $0 Your plan $ Over / Under: $0 Miscellaneous Stationery, favors, transport, license & more Recommended: $0 Your plan $ Over / Under: $0 Total recommended: $0 Your planned total: $0 Remaining / Over budget: $0 Print my budget Print or save as PDF to share with your partner, planner, or family.
How this wedding budget calculator works Behind the scenes, we’re using common industry guidelines for how couples typically split wedding costs.
From there, you can customize every category to match what matters most to you.
Start with your total number Enter the maximum amount you’re comfortable spending on your entire wedding.
This includes everything: venue, food, outfits, travel, license, and the little details that sneak up at the end.

Use smart default percentages Our starting percentages are based on typical wedding budgets: the venue and catering usually take the biggest slices, with photography, attire, rings, and miscellaneous costs filling in the rest.
You can tweak any category instantly.
Adjust and compare in real time As you type in your planned numbers, the calculator shows whether you’re over or under in each category.
You’ll see progress bars, totals, and how much you have left overall.
Print or save a shareable summary Once you’re happy with your plan, tap “Print my budget” to get a clean, printer-friendly summary.
Save it as a PDF or bring it to venue tours, catering calls, and dress appointments.
Make decisions together, not alone A wedding budget works best when both partners feel heard.
Use this calculator as a neutral starting point for conversations about trade-offs: what to scale back, where to splurge, and which details you can happily skip.
Many couples find it helpful to schedule a short “budget check-in” each week while planning.
Reviewing the numbers regularly keeps small changes from turning into big surprises.
Create a simple rhythm for money talks A budget is only helpful if it is revisited.
Building a light routine around your numbers keeps planning from turning into last‑minute panic.
Weekly 15‑minute check‑in Pick one evening each week to look at your budget together.
Update any new quotes, contracts, or changes to the guest list.
Keeping the ritual short makes it sustainable, even when life is busy.
One owner, shared decisions It often helps to nominate one person to keep the numbers tidy while both partners still weigh in on major choices.
That way, nothing falls through the cracks and no one feels shut out of the process.
Document your trade‑offs When you decide to spend more on one category and less on another, jot down why.
Those notes make it easier to feel confident later, because you can see the logic behind each adjustment instead of second‑guessing yourself.
See how different wedding budgets can look There is no single “right” number for a wedding.
What matters is that the budget you choose matches your income, region, and comfort level with spending.

Small celebration, big meaning A backyard ceremony with 35 guests, home-cooked food from family recipes, and a photographer for a few focused hours might prioritize intimacy and storytelling over a large reception.
The budget leans into memories and connection instead of formality.
Classic venue with tight guardrails A traditional banquet hall reception for 120 guests may work well when you cap add-ons, keep décor simple, and choose a menu that balances taste with cost.
Using the calculator to pre-approve upgrades prevents small extras from quietly expanding the total.
Destination weekend with fewer guests A smaller guest list in a meaningful destination can shift money toward travel, shared experiences, and welcome events.
The numbers may look different on paper, but the emotional return on investment can be high if it reflects what you truly value.
Money-saving wedding budget tips Every dollar you save on logistics is a dollar you can put toward experiences, memories, or your honeymoon.
Use these ideas as a starting point.
Prioritize your top three must-haves Decide what matters most: venue aesthetic, photography quality, live band, food, or guest count.
It’s okay to go above the recommended percentage in your top categories if you trim others to compensate.
Trim the guest list before anything else The fastest way to reduce costs is to lower your guest count.
Fewer guests means a smaller venue, less catering, fewer rentals, and often a more intimate, meaningful experience.
Ask vendors about off-peak discounts Many venues and photographers offer lower rates for weekday weddings, winter dates, or mornings.
If your schedule is flexible, these savings can be substantial.
Bundle services where it makes sense Some venues include tables, chairs, linens, and basic décor.
Some photographers also offer videography or engagement shoots in a package.
Bundles can simplify planning and save money.
Leave a real buffer for surprises Last-minute fees (service charges, overtime, transportation, alterations) appear more often than couples expect.
Keep at least 5–10% of your budget labeled as “miscellaneous” so you’re not stressed on the week of the wedding.
Think long-term, not just one day It’s okay to say no to trends that don’t matter to you.
Choose elements you’ll still love in photos and memories ten years from now—your future self will thank you.
Shift budget into memories, not things When making tough calls, ask yourselves which option you will remember most clearly ten years from now.
Experiences like a live musician for the ceremony or a relaxed morning-after brunch may matter more than favors or elaborate programs that guests quickly forget.
Looking at your budget through this “future memories” lens can make it easier to cut costs that do not truly add joy for you or your guests.
Use ranges instead of single numbers Instead of telling yourself “The dress must cost exactly this amount,” plan a realistic range.
Ranges give you room to say yes to a perfect‑fit option or to celebrate if you come in under your target without feeling like you “failed” at budgeting.
Plan for energy, not just money Some choices are inexpensive on paper but very demanding in time and energy—like heavy DIY projects close to the wedding.
As you assign dollars, also ask how much capacity you truly have to execute each idea without burning out.
Turn your budget into simple checklists Once your numbers are in place, translating them into short, focused checklists makes planning days feel less overwhelming and more achievable.
Checklist for this month Choose three money-related tasks that matter most right now—such as confirming a venue quote, deciding on a guest-count range, or researching photographers in your budget.
Write them down, complete them, and then move on to the next set of actions.
Checklist before each major payment Before you send a large deposit, pause and review how that amount fits into your overall plan.
Confirm you have accounted for taxes, service charges, and any extras you are likely to add later.
Checklist for protecting your buffer Create a short list of situations that do justify dipping into your flex fund—such as weather changes or accessibility needs—and situations that do not.
This makes it easier to say no to impulse upgrades that could strain your budget.
Wedding budget FAQ A few common questions couples ask when they start planning with a realistic budget.
Do we have to follow these exact percentages?
The percentages are just a starting point based on typical weddings.
If photography and video are more important to you than florals or favors, you can increase that category and lower others until your total fits your budget.
Is this calculator financial advice?
This tool is for educational and planning purposes only and does not replace personal financial advice.
Always consider your savings, income, and other responsibilities before finalizing any big expenses.
Can we use this for destination weddings?
The categories still apply, but your “miscellaneous” and “travel”-related spend may be higher.
Feel free to increase the misc percentage or rename that category in your printed budget.
What about other wedding costs like flowers or music?
Many of those expenses are included in the Miscellaneous category by default.
If you prefer more detail, you can split your misc total on paper into sub-categories such as flowers, DJ or band, stationery, transportation, and décor.
How often should we update our budget?
As a rule of thumb, review your numbers whenever you sign a new contract, adjust your guest list, or change venues or dates.
Updating your plan in small steps is much easier than trying to rebuild it from memory a few weeks before the wedding.
Should we track every tiny purchase?
You do not need to micro‑track every ribbon and candle for the budget to work.
Focus on the categories that move the needle—venue, catering, photo, attire, décor.
For small extras, consider grouping them under a single “incidentals” line so they are noted but not stressful.
How do we handle different money styles?
It is normal for partners to have different comfort levels with spending.
Use the budget as neutral ground: let one person lead on structure and the other lead on experience.
Agree on a few “non‑negotiable” items for each of you so the final plan feels fair and balanced.
How detailed should our categories be?
You can always split a category later if you find that it needs more detail.
For many couples, grouping smaller line items together—like signage, small décor, and candles—keeps the budget readable while still capturing the full picture of spending.

How this wedding budget calculator works Behind the scenes, we’re using common industry guidelines for how couples typically split wedding costs.
From there, you can customize every category to match what matters most to you.
Start with your total number Enter the maximum amount you’re comfortable spending on your entire wedding.
This includes everything: venue, food, outfits, travel, license, and the little details that sneak up at the end.

Use smart default percentages Our starting percentages are based on typical wedding budgets: the venue and catering usually take the biggest slices, with photography, attire, rings, and miscellaneous costs filling in the rest.
You can tweak any category instantly.
Adjust and compare in real time As you type in your planned numbers, the calculator shows whether you’re over or under in each category.
You’ll see progress bars, totals, and how much you have left overall.
Print or save a shareable summary Once you’re happy with your plan, tap “Print my budget” to get a clean, printer-friendly summary.
Save it as a PDF or bring it to venue tours, catering calls, and dress appointments.
Make decisions together, not alone A wedding budget works best when both partners feel heard.
Use this calculator as a neutral starting point for conversations about trade-offs: what to scale back, where to splurge, and which details you can happily skip.
Many couples find it helpful to schedule a short “budget check-in” each week while planning.
Reviewing the numbers regularly keeps small changes from turning into big surprises.
Create a simple rhythm for money talks A budget is only helpful if it is revisited.
Building a light routine around your numbers keeps planning from turning into last‑minute panic.
Weekly 15‑minute check‑in Pick one evening each week to look at your budget together.
Update any new quotes, contracts, or changes to the guest list.
Keeping the ritual short makes it sustainable, even when life is busy.
One owner, shared decisions It often helps to nominate one person to keep the numbers tidy while both partners still weigh in on major choices.
That way, nothing falls through the cracks and no one feels shut out of the process.
Document your trade‑offs When you decide to spend more on one category and less on another, jot down why.
Those notes make it easier to feel confident later, because you can see the logic behind each adjustment instead of second‑guessing yourself.
See how different wedding budgets can look There is no single “right” number for a wedding.
What matters is that the budget you choose matches your income, region, and comfort level with spending.

Small celebration, big meaning A backyard ceremony with 35 guests, home-cooked food from family recipes, and a photographer for a few focused hours might prioritize intimacy and storytelling over a large reception.
The budget leans into memories and connection instead of formality.
Classic venue with tight guardrails A traditional banquet hall reception for 120 guests may work well when you cap add-ons, keep décor simple, and choose a menu that balances taste with cost.
Using the calculator to pre-approve upgrades prevents small extras from quietly expanding the total.
Destination weekend with fewer guests A smaller guest list in a meaningful destination can shift money toward travel, shared experiences, and welcome events.
The numbers may look different on paper, but the emotional return on investment can be high if it reflects what you truly value.
Money-saving wedding budget tips Every dollar you save on logistics is a dollar you can put toward experiences, memories, or your honeymoon.
Use these ideas as a starting point.
Prioritize your top three must-haves Decide what matters most: venue aesthetic, photography quality, live band, food, or guest count.
It’s okay to go above the recommended percentage in your top categories if you trim others to compensate.
Trim the guest list before anything else The fastest way to reduce costs is to lower your guest count.
Fewer guests means a smaller venue, less catering, fewer rentals, and often a more intimate, meaningful experience.
Ask vendors about off-peak discounts Many venues and photographers offer lower rates for weekday weddings, winter dates, or mornings.
If your schedule is flexible, these savings can be substantial.
Bundle services where it makes sense Some venues include tables, chairs, linens, and basic décor.
Some photographers also offer videography or engagement shoots in a package.
Bundles can simplify planning and save money.
Leave a real buffer for surprises Last-minute fees (service charges, overtime, transportation, alterations) appear more often than couples expect.
Keep at least 5–10% of your budget labeled as “miscellaneous” so you’re not stressed on the week of the wedding.
Think long-term, not just one day It’s okay to say no to trends that don’t matter to you.
Choose elements you’ll still love in photos and memories ten years from now—your future self will thank you.
Shift budget into memories, not things When making tough calls, ask yourselves which option you will remember most clearly ten years from now.
Experiences like a live musician for the ceremony or a relaxed morning-after brunch may matter more than favors or elaborate programs that guests quickly forget.
Looking at your budget through this “future memories” lens can make it easier to cut costs that do not truly add joy for you or your guests.
Use ranges instead of single numbers Instead of telling yourself “The dress must cost exactly this amount,” plan a realistic range.
Ranges give you room to say yes to a perfect‑fit option or to celebrate if you come in under your target without feeling like you “failed” at budgeting.
Plan for energy, not just money Some choices are inexpensive on paper but very demanding in time and energy—like heavy DIY projects close to the wedding.
As you assign dollars, also ask how much capacity you truly have to execute each idea without burning out.
Turn your budget into simple checklists Once your numbers are in place, translating them into short, focused checklists makes planning days feel less overwhelming and more achievable.
Checklist for this month Choose three money-related tasks that matter most right now—such as confirming a venue quote, deciding on a guest-count range, or researching photographers in your budget.
Write them down, complete them, and then move on to the next set of actions.
Checklist before each major payment Before you send a large deposit, pause and review how that amount fits into your overall plan.
Confirm you have accounted for taxes, service charges, and any extras you are likely to add later.
Checklist for protecting your buffer Create a short list of situations that do justify dipping into your flex fund—such as weather changes or accessibility needs—and situations that do not.
This makes it easier to say no to impulse upgrades that could strain your budget.
Wedding budget FAQ A few common questions couples ask when they start planning with a realistic budget.
Do we have to follow these exact percentages?
The percentages are just a starting point based on typical weddings.
If photography and video are more important to you than florals or favors, you can increase that category and lower others until your total fits your budget.
Is this calculator financial advice?
This tool is for educational and planning purposes only and does not replace personal financial advice.
Always consider your savings, income, and other responsibilities before finalizing any big expenses.
Can we use this for destination weddings?
The categories still apply, but your “miscellaneous” and “travel”-related spend may be higher.
Feel free to increase the misc percentage or rename that category in your printed budget.
What about other wedding costs like flowers or music?
Many of those expenses are included in the Miscellaneous category by default.
If you prefer more detail, you can split your misc total on paper into sub-categories such as flowers, DJ or band, stationery, transportation, and décor.
How often should we update our budget?
As a rule of thumb, review your numbers whenever you sign a new contract, adjust your guest list, or change venues or dates.
Updating your plan in small steps is much easier than trying to rebuild it from memory a few weeks before the wedding.
Should we track every tiny purchase?
You do not need to micro‑track every ribbon and candle for the budget to work.
Focus on the categories that move the needle—venue, catering, photo, attire, décor.
For small extras, consider grouping them under a single “incidentals” line so they are noted but not stressful.
How do we handle different money styles?
It is normal for partners to have different comfort levels with spending.
Use the budget as neutral ground: let one person lead on structure and the other lead on experience.
Agree on a few “non‑negotiable” items for each of you so the final plan feels fair and balanced.
How detailed should our categories be?
You can always split a category later if you find that it needs more detail.
For many couples, grouping smaller line items together—like signage, small décor, and candles—keeps the budget readable while still capturing the full picture of spending.
